title: Structural Change within the Services Sector and the Future of Cost Disease
journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
abstract: Abstract In his seminal work, Baumol observed that developed economies suffer from cost disease, i.e., aggregate productivity growth falls because structural change reallocates production to services with low productivity growth. We document that cost disease importantly contributed to the productivity growth slowdown in the postwar U.S. To assess how severe cost disease may become, we build a model of structural change among the goods sector and broad services sectors. Calibrating the model to the postwar U.S. implies that broad categories of services are substitutes and the services with low productivity growth do not take over production. Simulating the calibrated model forward implies that future cost disease will be less severe than the past one.
summary_en: Baumol's cost disease — the idea that rich economies slow down because growth shifts production to low-productivity services — has been a staple worry for decades. This paper asks how severe the problem will become going forward. Using a structural-change model of the goods and broad-services sectors calibrated to post-war US data, the authors find that low-productivity services are substitutes for higher-productivity ones, not complements. That means they don't take over production; they get substituted away. Simulating the model forward, cost disease in the future will be less severe than in the past. A reassuring result for long-run productivity forecasts.
data_used: Theoretical structural-change model calibrated to post-war US sectoral data: value-added shares and productivity growth rates for goods and broad services categories.
policy_relevance: For Hungary's long-run growth forecasting (MNB, Pénzügyminisztérium, KSH) and for retirement-system projections that depend on productivity growth, the paper is a mildly optimistic counter-point to cost-disease pessimism: automation and substitution within services will limit the drag. Relevant to fiscal-sustainability modelling and productivity assumptions in pension projections. Caveat: the calibration is to US data; the Hungarian services mix and productivity gap with the frontier differ, and a Hungarian re-calibration might yield different magnitudes.
